# Model Fitting Exercise

## Load Data
First we start by loading our data about the drug Mavoglurant.
```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(here)

#Load our data
drug_data <- read_csv(here("fitting-exercise", "Mavoglurant_A2121_nmpk.csv"))
glimpse(drug_data)
```

The dataset contains 2,678 observations and 17 variables. Each row represents a measurement at a specific time point for a participant. Key variables include ID for participant identification, DV for measured drug concentration, TIME for when the measurement occurred, DOSE for administered dose, and OCC for dosing occasion. Participant characteristics such as AGE, SEX, RACE, WT (weight), and HT (height) are also included. 

## Plot drug concentration over time for each individual
```{r}
ggplot(drug_data, aes(x = TIME, y = DV, group = ID, color = factor(DOSE))) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3) +
  labs(
    title = "Drug Concentration Over Time",
    x = "Time",
    y = "Drug Concentration (DV)",
    color = "Dose"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

### Facet by Dose
Here we separate each dosing into a separate graph for easier readability with the `facet_wrap` function.

```{r}
ggplot(drug_data, aes(x = TIME, y = DV, group = ID, color = factor(DOSE))) +
  facet_wrap(~ DOSE) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3) +
  labs(
    title = "Drug Concentration Over Time",
    x = "Time",
    y = "Drug Concentration (DV)",
    color = "Dose"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

This plot shows drug concentration (DV) over time for each participant. Each line represents one individual, and colors indicate different dose levels. Overall, higher doses appear to produce higher drug concentrations over time.

## Keep only the first dosing occasion
The dataset contains an indicator variable OCC representing dosing occasions. Some individuals received the drug more than once (OCC = 1 and OCC = 2). Since the difference between occasions is unclear and we want to avoid complications from repeated dosing, I retain only observations where OCC = 1. This ensures that the data from the participants, is from only one dosing occasion.
```{r}
drug_occ1 <- drug_data %>%
  filter(OCC == 1)

glimpse(drug_occ1)
```

## Remove dosing rows and compute total drug exposure
```{r}
y_data <- drug_occ1 %>%
  filter(TIME != 0) %>%
  group_by(ID) %>%
  summarize(Y = sum(DV), .groups = "drop")

dim(y_data)
```

To summarize drug exposure for each participant, total drug concentration across was calcualtes across measured time points. This value is labeled as Y, and represents drug concentration for each individual.

```{r}
# Keep the dosing rows, (TIME = 0)
dose_rows <- drug_occ1 %>%
  filter(TIME == 0)

dim(dose_rows)
```

Observations where TIME = 0 correspond to the dosing event for each individual. These rows contain participant characteristics such as dose, age, sex, race, weight, and height. I extract these rows so they can later be merged with the total (calculated) drug exposure variable.

## Merge exposure with participant characteristics
```{r}
# Drug exposure with the participant characteristics
analysis_data <- left_join(y_data, dose_rows, by = "ID")

dim(analysis_data)
```

```{r}
analysis_data <- analysis_data %>%
  mutate(
    SEX = factor(SEX),
    RACE = factor(RACE)
  ) %>%
  select(Y, DOSE, AGE, SEX, RACE, WT, HT)

glimpse(analysis_data)
```

The total exposure variables, labled Y, is merged with participant characteristics by participant ID and create a dataset where each row represents a participant with their demographic characteristic and total drug exposure.

## Summary Statistics 
I begin by examining summary statistics for the cleaned dataset to understand the general distribution of the variables and check for unusual values.
```{r}
summary(analysis_data)
```

# Exploratory Analysis (EDA)
```{r}
# Histogram
ggplot(analysis_data, aes(x = Y)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Total Drug Concentration")
```

The histogram displays the distribution of total drug exposure (Y). The distribution appears to be skewed right, which is common for pharmacokinetic measurements where some individuals experience higher exposure than others. Most participants exposure values between ~1700 and ~3050.

```{r}
# Scatterplot by dose
ggplot(analysis_data, aes(x = DOSE, y = Y)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Total Drug Exposure vs Dose")
```

This scatterplot looks at the relationship between dose and total drug exposure. Higher doses tend to correspond with higher total exposure, indicating a positive relationship between dose and total drug exposure.

```{r}
# Scatterplot by age 
ggplot(analysis_data, aes(x = AGE, y = Y)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal()
```

This plot looks whether age is associated with total drug exposure. There does not appear to be a strong relationship between age and total drug exposure in this dataset as the results are extremely varibale.

```{r}
# pairs plots
pairs(analysis_data[, c("Y","DOSE","AGE","WT","HT")])
```

The pairwise plots show that dose appears to have the strongest relationship with total drug exposure (Y),
while other variables such as age, weight, and height show weaker relationships with (Y)

# Model Fitting
Now we will focus on more modeling fitting for this Mavogulrant dataset.

## Linear Model with Y - DOSE
```{r}
# Linear regression with Y as outcome and DOSE as predictor
lm_fit <- lm(Y ~ DOSE, data = analysis_data)
summary(lm_fit)  # shows coefficients and R-squared
```

This linear regression examines the relationship between dose (DOSE) and total drug exposure (Y). 
The estimated coefficient for dose is 58.2, meaning that for each additional unit increase in dose, total drug exposure increases by approximately 58 units on average. The model explains roughly 52% of the variation in exposure (R² = 0.516), and the F-test for the overall model is highly significant (F = 125.6, p < 0.001), indicating that dose is a meaningful predictor of total drug exposure in this dataset. The residuals suggest moderate variability around the fitted values, so while dose is important, other factors may also contribute to differences in exposure.

## Linear Model with Y - All Predictors 
```{r}
# Linear regression with Y as outcome and all predictors
lm_all <- lm(Y ~ DOSE + AGE + SEX + RACE + WT + HT, data = analysis_data)
summary(lm_all)
```

This linear regression model includes dose with additional characteristics (age, sex, race, weight, and height) to predict total drug exposure. The model explains approximately 61.9% of the variation in total drug exposure, higher than the model including only dose. The overall model is statistically significant (F=22.57, p < 0.001) which sugest that the predictors altogether help to explain variation in drug exposure.

```{r}
# Compute RMSE and R-squared for the DOSE model
pred_lm <- predict(lm_fit, analysis_data)
rmse_lm <- yardstick::rmse_vec(truth = analysis_data$Y, estimate = pred_lm)
rsq_lm <- yardstick::rsq_vec(truth = analysis_data$Y, estimate = pred_lm)

rmse_lm
rsq_lm

# Y ~ All Predictors
pred_lm_all <- predict(lm_all, analysis_data)
rmse_lm_all <- yardstick::rmse_vec(truth=analysis_data$Y, estimate=pred_lm_all)
rsq_lm_all <- yardstick::rsq_vec(truth=analysis_data$Y, estimate=pred_lm_all)

rmse_lm_all
rsq_lm_all

```

The model using only DOSE has an RMSE of 666.5 and explains about 51.6% of the variation in total drug exposure (R² = 0.516). When all predictors are included, the RMSE decreases to 590.9 and the R² increases to 0.619. This indicates that adding AGE, SEX, RACE, WT, and HT improves the model’s ability to predict drug exposure. Overall, the full model provides a better fit than the dose-only model.

## Logistic Model with SEX - DOSE 
```{r}
# Logistic regression with SEX as outcome and DOSE as predictor
log_fit <- glm(SEX ~ DOSE, data = analysis_data, family = binomial)
summary(log_fit)
```

This logistic regression model looks at whether drug dose predicts participant sex. The coefficient for dose is not statistically significant (p = 0.192), indicating that dose is not associated with sex in this dataset. Additionally, the small change between the null deviance and residual deviance suggests that dose does not substantially improve the model's ability to predict sex. However, this can be expected as drug dose is not related to participant sex. 

```{r}
# Predicted probabilities
pred_prob <- predict(log_fit, analysis_data, type = "response")

# Convert to predicted classes
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_prob > 0.5, 2, 1)
pred_class <- factor(pred_class, levels = c(1, 2))
sex_factor <- factor(analysis_data$SEX, levels = c(1, 2))

# Accuracy
accuracy_log <- yardstick::accuracy_vec(
  truth = sex_factor,
  estimate = pred_class
)

accuracy_log

# ROC-AUC
roc_auc_log <- yardstick::roc_auc_vec(
  truth = sex_factor,
  estimate = pred_prob
)
roc_auc_log
```

ROC-AUC evaluates how well the model distinguishes between the two categories of sex. A value close to 0.5 suggests that the model performs little better than random guessing. 


## Logistic Model with SEX - All Predictors
```{r}
# Logistic regression with SEX as outcome and all predictors
log_all <- glm(SEX ~ DOSE + AGE + RACE + WT + HT + Y, data = analysis_data, family = binomial)
summary(log_all)
```

This logistic regression model includes dose, age, race, weight, height, and total drug exposure (Y) to predict participant sex. Most predictors are not statistically significant, indicating that these variables do not strongly explain differences in sex in this dataset. Height is the only predictor that appears statistically significant (p = 0.0027), indicating it may be associated with sex. Overall, many predictors do not contribute to predicting sex.

```{r}
library(yardstick)

# Predicted probabilities for full logistic model
pred_prob_all <- predict(log_all, analysis_data, type = "response")

# Convert to predicted classes using 0.5 cutoff
pred_class_all <- ifelse(pred_prob_all > 0.5, 2, 1)
pred_class_all <- factor(pred_class_all, levels = c(1, 2))

# Convert true SEX to factor
sex_factor <- factor(analysis_data$SEX, levels = c(1, 2))

# Accuracy
accuracy_log_all <- yardstick::accuracy_vec(
  truth = sex_factor,
  estimate = pred_class_all
)
accuracy_log_all

# ROC AUC
roc_auc_log_all <- yardstick::roc_auc_vec(
  truth = sex_factor,
  estimate = pred_prob_all
)
roc_auc_log_all
```

The full logistic model predicts sex with high accuracy (94.2%), meaning most participants are classified correctly. However, the ROC-AUC is very low (0.02), indicating the model performs poorly at distinguishing between males and females. This mismatch occurs because the dataset is heavily imbalanced (many more males than females). Essentially, while the model “guesses” correctly most of the time, it does not truly capture differences between the sexes.

## Prepare Data for Modeling
```{r}
rngseed <- 1234
```

For this exercise, the RACE variable is removed due to irreuglar values. The dataset now contains 120 observations and six variables: Y, DOSE, AGE, SEX, WT, and HT. There are now 120 observations in the dataset.
```{r}
analysis_data2 <- analysis_data %>%
  select(Y, DOSE, AGE, SEX, WT, HT)

  glimpse(analysis_data2)
```

```{r}
set.seed(rngseed)

data_split <- initial_split(analysis_data2, prop = 0.75)

train_data <- training(data_split)
test_data  <- testing(data_split)

dim(train_data)
dim(test_data)
```

The data are randomly split into a training set (75%) and a test set (25%). The training data will be used to fit models, while the test data will be used for the final evaluation.

## Model Fitting Using Training Data

### Model 1: DOSE Only
```{r}
model1_spec <- linear_reg() %>%
  set_engine("lm")

model1_fit <- model1_spec %>%
  fit(Y ~ DOSE, data = train_data)

model1_fit
```

### Model 2: All Predictors
```{r}
model2_spec <- linear_reg() %>%
  set_engine("lm")

model2_fit <- model2_spec %>%
  fit(Y ~ DOSE + AGE + SEX + WT + HT, data = train_data)

model2_fit
```

## Model Performance on Training Data
To evaluate model performance on the training data, predictions are generated for each model and compared with the observed values of total drug exposure (Y). The root mean squared error (RMSE) measures the average magnitude of prediction error, with lower values indicating better predictive accuracy. By comparing RMSE values across models, we can assess whether including additional predictors improves the model's ability to explain variation in drug exposure.
```{r}
pred1 <- predict(model1_fit, train_data) %>%
  bind_cols(train_data)

pred2 <- predict(model2_fit, train_data) %>%
  bind_cols(train_data)

rmse_model1 <- rmse(pred1, truth = Y, estimate = .pred)
rmse_model2 <- rmse(pred2, truth = Y, estimate = .pred)

rmse_model1
rmse_model2
```

The model using dose as the only predictor produces an RMSE of approximately 703. When additional participant characteristics (AGE, SEX, WT, and HT) are included, the RMSE decreases to about 627. This reduction in prediction error suggests that incorporating variables improves the model's ability to predict total drug exposure compared with using dose alone.

## Null Model
To provide a baseline for comparison, a null model is constructed. The null model predicts total drug exposure for every participant using only the mean value of Y from the training data. This model contains no predictors and represents the simplest possible prediction approach. Comparing the RMSE of the fitted models to the null model allows us to determine whether the predictors meaningfully improve prediction accuracy.

```{r}
mean_y <- mean(train_data$Y)

null_pred <- rep(mean_y, nrow(train_data))

rmse_null <- yardstick::rmse_vec(
  truth = train_data$Y,
  estimate = null_pred
)

rmse_null
```

The null model produces an RMSE of about 948, meaning predictions based only on the mean outcome perform poorly. This indicates that models including predictors such as dose and participant characteristics provide much more accurate predictions than simply using the average exposure value for all individuals.

## Cross-Validation
Although RMSE calculated on the training data provides an estimate of model fit, it may underestimate error because the models were evaluated on the same data used for fitting. To better assess how well the models generalize to new data, 10-fold cross-validation is performed. In this procedure, the training data are divided into ten subsets (folds). The model is repeatedly trained on nine folds and evaluated on the remaining fold, and this process is repeated until each fold has served as the validation set.

```{r}
set.seed(rngseed)

cv_folds <- vfold_cv(train_data, v = 10)
```

### Fit Models with Cross-Validation

```{r}
model1_cv <- fit_resamples(
  model1_spec,
  Y ~ DOSE,
  resamples = cv_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)

model2_cv <- fit_resamples(
  model2_spec,
  Y ~ DOSE + AGE + SEX + WT + HT,
  resamples = cv_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)
```

### Cross-Validation Results

```{r}
collect_metrics(model1_cv)
collect_metrics(model2_cv)
```

The cross-validation results provide an estimate of model prediction error on unseen, new data. The dose-only model has a cross-validated RMSE of approximately 691, while the full model including additional predictors has a lower RMSE of about 646. These results are consistent with the training RMSE values and suggest that the full model provides better predictive performance while maintaining reasonable generalizability.

### Changing the Random Seed
```{r}
rngseed <- 4321
set.seed(rngseed)

cv_folds2 <- vfold_cv(train_data, v = 10)
```

**This section added by Aylla von Ermland**

Creating predictions for all 3 models (training data):

model1_fit (DOSE only)
model2_fit (all predictors)
mean_y (null model)

```{r}
# Model 1 predictions (DOSE only)
pred_m1 <- predict(model1_fit, train_data) %>%
  bind_cols(train_data %>% select(Y)) %>%
  mutate(model = "DOSE only")

# Model 2 predictions (all predictors)
pred_m2 <- predict(model2_fit, train_data) %>%
  bind_cols(train_data %>% select(Y)) %>%
  mutate(model = "All predictors")

# Null model predictions
pred_null <- train_data %>%
  mutate(
    .pred = mean_y,
    model = "Null model"
  ) %>%
  select(Y, .pred, model)
```

Combine into one dataset and plotting observed vs predicted:

```{r}
pred_all <- bind_rows(pred_m1, pred_m2, pred_null)

ggplot(pred_all, aes(x = Y, y = .pred, color = model)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 5000), ylim = c(0, 5000)) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed vs predicted values",
    x = "Observed (Y)",
    y = "Predicted",
    color = "Model"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

The null model produces a horizontal line because all predictions are equal to the mean outcome. The dose-only model produces three horizontal bands corresponding to the discrete dose levels, indicating that predictions depend only on dose. The model including all predictors performs best, with points more closely aligned to the 45-degree line, although substantial variability remains. Notably, the model tends to underpredict higher observed values, suggesting potential model misspecification or nonlinear relationships.

**Model predictions and uncertainty:**

```{r}
set.seed(rngseed)

boot_data <- bootstraps(train_data, times = 100)
```

```{r}
library(purrr)

# Generate predictions for model 2 on training data
pred_model2 <- predict(model2_fit, train_data) %>%
  bind_cols(train_data)

# Compute residuals
residual_df <- pred_model2 %>%
  mutate(residual = .pred - Y)

# Plot residuals vs predicted
ggplot(residual_df, aes(x = .pred, y = residual)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, color = "black") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-2500, 2500)) +
  labs(
    title = "Residuals vs predicted Values (model 2)",
    x = "Predicted values",
    y = "Residuals"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

The residuals are pretty spread out, so the model isn’t super precise. The model isn’t fully capturing the pattern in the data and might be missing something or be too simple.

```{r}
library(rsample)

set.seed(rngseed)

# Create bootstrap samples
boot_obj <- bootstraps(train_data, times = 100)

# Pre-allocate matrix
pred_bs <- matrix(NA, nrow = 100, ncol = nrow(train_data))

# Loop through bootstrap samples
for (i in seq_len(100)) {
  
  boot_sample <- analysis(boot_obj$splits[[i]])
  
  # Fit model using lm (safe + numeric output)
  fit_boot <- lm(Y ~ DOSE + AGE + SEX + WT + HT, data = boot_sample)
  
  # Store predictions 
  pred_bs[i, ] <- predict(fit_boot, newdata = train_data)
}
```

Compute uncertainty:
```{r}
# Quantiles: 89% CI + median
pred_summary <- apply(pred_bs, 2, quantile, probs = c(0.055, 0.5, 0.945))

pred_summary <- t(pred_summary)
pred_summary <- as.data.frame(pred_summary)

colnames(pred_summary) <- c("lower", "median", "upper")
```

Combine with original predictors:
```{r}
# Original predictions (tidymodels → extract .pred)
orig_pred <- predict(model2_fit, train_data)$.pred

plot_df <- train_data %>%
  mutate(
    pred_point = orig_pred,
    lower = pred_summary$lower,
    median = pred_summary$median,
    upper = pred_summary$upper
  )
```

Plot for uncertainty visualization:
```{r}
ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = Y)) +
  geom_point(aes(y = pred_point), color = "black", alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_point(aes(y = median), color = "black", alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), color = "black", alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 5000), ylim = c(0, 5000)) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed vs predicted with Bootstrap",
    x = "Observed values",
    y = "Predicted values"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

The model does okay overall since the points follow the diagonal, but there’s still a lot of scatter so it’s not super accurate. The error bars get bigger at higher values, so it’s less reliable there. You can also see it tends to underpredict the bigger values since a lot of points fall below the line.

# Part 3 
We start by loading the dataset and preparing it for analysis. This section includes evaluating model performance to assess how well our models explain the outcome and how they generalize to new data.
```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)

# Predict on test data using your previously fitted model2
pred_test <- predict(model2_fit, new_data = test_data) %>%
  bind_cols(test_data)

# Predict on training data again for comparison
pred_train <- predict(model2_fit, new_data = train_data) %>%
  bind_cols(train_data)
```

```{r}
all_preds <- bind_rows(
  pred_train %>% mutate(dataset = "Train"),
  pred_test %>% mutate(dataset = "Test")
)

# Plot predicted vs observed
ggplot(all_preds, aes(x = .pred, y = Y, color = dataset, shape = dataset)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Predicted Y", y = "Observed Y", 
       title = "Predicted vs Observed: Training vs Test Data") +
  theme_minimal()
```

The predicted vs observed plot shows that both training and test data point generlly follow the 45-degree line, indicating that the model capture the overall relationship between predictors and drug exposure. The similar spread between trainng and test data suggest that generalization is reasonable despite the increase in variability at higher values.

```{r}
rmse_train <- rmse(pred_train, truth = Y, estimate = .pred)
rmse_test  <- rmse(pred_test, truth = Y, estimate = .pred)

rmse_train
rmse_test
```

The RMSE for the training data (520) is lower than the test data (627), indicating that the model performs better on than the data it was trained on. The increase in RMSE for the test data suggests some loss of accuracy on the new data. 

```{r}
# Save the cleaned dataset for the ML exercise
saveRDS(analysis_data2, "clean_data.rds")
```
